critique.summary: The article reports that North Korea operates a large-scale fake IT worker scheme, employing around 100,000 individuals generating approximately $500 million annually for the regime. It details the organizational structure, recruitment tactics, and potential mitigation strategies for companies to avoid falling victim.
mainPoints: North Korea's fake IT worker operation involves about 100,000 individuals earning over $300,000 annually each, collectively generating $500 million per year.
The scheme includes recruiters, facilitators, IT workers, and Western collaborators, often using fake identities and tools like VPNs and counterfeit accounts.
The report highlights methods of detection and mitigation, such as recognizing fake backgrounds and discrepancies during interviews.
critique.truthfulness: The article cites research from IBM X-Force and Flare Research, and references US government data, suggesting a credible basis for the claims. However, specific figures like the number of workers and total earnings are difficult to verify independently, and some details may be based on intelligence estimates or extrapolations. The overall depiction aligns with known North Korean cyber and economic activities, but precise numbers should be treated with caution.
considerations: The article does not specify the sources of the US government data or whether these figures are estimates or confirmed intelligence.
It does not address the potential for overstatement or underestimation of the scheme's scale, which is common in intelligence reports.
The effectiveness of proposed mitigation strategies in real-world scenarios remains uncertain.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the nature of North Korea's alleged 'fake' IT workers generating revenue for the regime, with debate on whether these workers are truly fake or real individuals using deceptive identities. Commenters also question the logistics, compensation, and corporate hiring practices that allow such schemes to persist.

comments.author: abtinf
comments.text:
I’m a little unclear on the usage of the word “fake” here.

Going by article, these are real people doing actual real work, they often use stolen identities to conceal information about themselves, and they get help from outside sources to do their jobs better.

Whatever the right word is, it’s not “fake”. Maybe fraudulent? Or ulterior motives? Or deceptive? Or pretext? Or threat actor? Or foreign agents?
comments.analysis: This comment accurately questions the terminology used in the article and clarifies that the workers are real people using deceptive means, which aligns with known tactics in such schemes.

comments.author: Barrin92
comments.text:
Genuinely baffled by the logistics of this. The article makes it sound like these are large numbers of people in NK or surrounding countries who rely on Google translate, so not sophisticated spies or whatever.

Even if they get their hands on a fake American ID, these are taxable, insured jobs, they're not working at a restaurant under the table. IT companies ship out hardware, where are these people banked etc?

How does this practically look, officially you're working with Mark Johnson but you end up on a zoom call with a guy who speaks broken English and connects from the other part of the world and you're not suspicious?
comments.analysis: Raises valid logistical concerns about how such workers operate remotely and how companies fail to detect them, highlighting practical challenges in the scenario.

comments.author: mlmonkey
comments.text:
A friend of mine got two such "fake" candidates for a coding interview. His experience reminded me of those "Nigerian Prince" emails from 20 years ago.
These two gentlemen had western names (like "Brandon Smith") but Asian features and a tenuous grasp of spoken English; even though they claimed to have undergrad degrees from US universities. And he could tell they were looking at another screen to copy code from.  After just a few minutes he realized what was going on, but continued the interview just to get the experience.
comments.analysis: Provides anecdotal evidence supporting the claim that some candidates use deceptive identities and tactics, which adds credibility though it is anecdotal.

comments.author: staplung
comments.text: The numbers in the headline seem odd. They imply that each (fake|fraudulent) worker only nets $5000 per year for Kim. I know the system has some inefficiencies where people behind the scenes are helping the "employee" with the work and there are cost of living expenses, taxes etc. but that seems like a pretty low take.
comments.analysis: Critically examines the financial figures presented, pointing out that the per-worker revenue seems low, which is a reasonable and relevant observation.

comments.author: OutOfHere
comments.text:
How is it that corporations can't get their act together wrt sensible hiring of remote workers? Before giving someone a final offer letter, why is it so difficult to meet them once (somewhere outside of North Korea and China)? The cost is negligible compared to a large salary.

What corporations actually do for verification also is equally damning. They ask for references, which no coworker really has an obligation to give, and it comes in the way of independent thought. Meanwhile, those from North Korea will sail through this blocker by having their fellow countrymen serve as references.
comments.analysis: Highlights corporate failures in verifying remote workers’ identities, which is a known issue and relevant to the discussion on how such schemes persist.

comments.author: woah
comments.text: How are these IT workers fake? Sounds like they are really doing the job.
comments.analysis: Concise and accurate in pointing out that the workers are real individuals performing real work, challenging the misleading use of 'fake'.

comments.author: paulpauper
comments.text: It's evident starting in 2017-2018 with the surge of the price of crypto and the rise of WFH with COVID, North Korea pivoted from rockets to much more lucrative and safer cyber theft to enrich its leadership and attack the West. A success. Policy makers don't care.
comments.analysis: Provides a broader geopolitical context linking North Korea’s cyber activities with global trends, which is plausible and supported by external reports.

comments.author: ck2
comments.text:
Actual atomic weapons not just stockpile, hundreds stave to death there daily, and everyone knows the famous satellite view of the entire country in darkness at night (while his palace is lit)

Yet no oil so they will be one of the longest surviving tyrannies in history

We can bet every country like them now will be building massive war drone factories too
comments.analysis: While somewhat tangential, this comment reflects common knowledge about North Korea’s conditions and military status, though it adds little to the IT worker discussion.

comments.author: narrator
comments.text:
North Korea runs like a big organized crime family that specializes in forced labor human trafficking and drugs. I've read that they even operate overseas businesses that send slaves that aren't allowed to leave those businesses such as for timber harvesting in the Russian far east and various businesses in South East Asia.

The Latin American cartels operate almost like miniature North Koreas.
comments.analysis: Makes a broad but generally accurate statement about North Korea’s illicit activities, though it is somewhat generalized and lacks direct connection to the article’s focus.

comments.author: nottorp
comments.text:
But are only North Koreans fake?

I got an offer to "lend" my resume/identity to an upwork profile for a couple hundred per ... week iirc. Or was it month?

It could have been NK but it could have also been any other country where that makes financial sense. Or someone running a bunch of "AI"s.
comments.analysis: Adds perspective that identity lending and fake profiles are not unique to North Korea, which is a reasonable and relevant point.
